Idiom (Cham): Dua koro gye mframa a, ɛbu.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Complex conditional clause; subject + verb + object + conditional marker; fixed proverbial construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A single person acting alone without support will eventually fail under pressure.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: One person cannot handle everything alone.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech, leadership discussions, family advice, workplace collaboration
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Forest-region proverb among Anyin communities in eastern Côte d’Ivoire and western Ghana emphasizing communal survival.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): dua kɔrɔ ɟe mframa a ɛbu
First Example Sentence: Wopɛ sɛ woyɛ adwuma no wo nko ara, na wo werɛ fi sɛ dua koro gye mframa a, ɛbu.
Second Example Sentence: Ɔpanin no kae sɛ dua koro gye mframa a, ɛbu bere a na abusua no regye akyinnye.
Third Example Sentence: Sɛ kuw no renka abom a, dua koro gye mframa a, ɛbu.

Idiom (Cham): Nsuo a ɛda fam no na ɛdɔ.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative clause structure; noun + relative marker + verb; fixed descriptive proverb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Quiet or humble people are often the most thoughtful or deep.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Quiet people often have deep thoughts.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from riverine life in Anyin territories where still water appears shallow but runs deep.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): nsuo a ɛda fam no na ɛdɔ
First Example Sentence: Ɔyɛ komm deɛ, nanso nsuo a ɛda fam no na ɛdɔ.
Second Example Sentence: Mma no nyinaa kasa bebree, na nsuo a ɛda fam no na ɛdɔ wɔ wɔn mu baako.
Third Example Sentence: Ɔkyerɛkyerɛni no kae sɛ nsuo a ɛda fam no na ɛdɔ.

Idiom (Cham): Se wo werɛ fi na wosankɔfa a, yenkyi.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional-reflexive clause; subordinate clause + main clause; fixed proverb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: It is not shameful to correct your mistakes.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: It is good to fix your mistakes.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Widely shared Akan-Anyin moral teaching emphasizing humility and accountability.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): sɛ wo werɛ fi na wosankɔfa a jɛŋkji
First Example Sentence: Sɛ wo werɛ fi na wosankɔfa a, yenkyi enti kɔsrɛ kyɛw.
Second Example Sentence: Ɔpanin no hyɛɛ no nkuran sɛ se wo werɛ fi na wosankɔfa a, yenkyi.
Third Example Sentence: Me ka kyerɛɛ no sɛ se wo werɛ fi na wosankɔfa a, yenkyi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Equative nominal clause; noun + copula (implicit) + noun; fixed metaphor.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects trading traditions of Anyin merchants in Bondoukou and surrounding markets.
First Example Sentence: Ɔkae sɛ sika ne mogya wɔ wiase yi mu.
Second Example Sentence: Adwuma no ho hia, efisɛ sika ne mogya.
Third Example Sentence: Wɔde ne ho hyɛɛ adwuma no mu paa, na ɔkae sɛ sika ne mogya.

Idiom (Cham): Anoma antu a, ɔbɔ ne ho ban.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative conditional clause; subject + negative verb + conditional marker.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: If one does not take initiative, nothing changes.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: You must act to protect yourself.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agrarian metaphor rooted in hunting and bird imagery common in Anyin oral tradition.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): anoma antu a ɔbɔ nɛ ho ban
First Example Sentence: Ɔka kyerɛɛ mmeranteɛ no sɛ anoma antu a, ɔbɔ ne ho ban.
Second Example Sentence: Bere a na kuw no retwentwɛn ne nan ase no, ɔpanin no kae sɛ anoma antu a, ɔbɔ ne ho ban.
Third Example Sentence: Mekae sɛ anoma antu a, ɔbɔ ne ho ban ansa na meyɛ adwuma no.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Simple declarative clause; subject + copula + noun phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Life is a long journey filled with stages.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Life has many stages and challenges.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common in Anyin funeral dirges and oral poetry traditions.
First Example Sentence: Ɔkyerɛkyerɛni no kae sɛ ɔbra yɛ akwantu tenten.
Second Example Sentence: Yɛhyehyɛɛ asɛm no ase sɛ ɔbra yɛ akwantu tenten.
Third Example Sentence: Ɔka kyerɛɛ ne mma sɛ ɔbra yɛ akwantu tenten.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; subject + verb + object; metaphorical proverb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A child does not think only one thought; children are curious and unpredictable.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in communal child-rearing practices of Anyin villages.
First Example Sentence: Mma no reyɛ basabasa, na mekae sɛ abofra bo nnwene baako.
Second Example Sentence: Ɔmaame no serew kae sɛ abofra bo nnwene baako.
Third Example Sentence: Ɔkyerɛkyerɛni no kae sɛ abofra bo nnwene baako bere a na mmofra no rebisa nsɛm pii.

Idiom (Cham): Akokɔ da ne ho adi anɔpa.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative clause; subject + verb phrase + temporal adverb.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Linked to rural life where roosters signal dawn and visibility.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Cham): akokɔ da nɛ ho adi anɔpa
First Example Sentence: Ɔkae sɛ akokɔ da ne ho adi anɔpa bere a asɛm no da adi.
Second Example Sentence: Sɛ woka atoro a, kae sɛ akokɔ da ne ho adi anɔpa.
Third Example Sentence: Yɛhwɛɛ akyiri no, na akokɔ da ne ho adi anɔpa.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Nominal juxtaposition; metaphorical pairing of nouns.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Two things that naturally complement each other.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Culinary metaphor common in Anyin households.
First Example Sentence: Wɔn baanu no te sɛ nkyene ne asaw.
Second Example Sentence: Ɔkae sɛ adwuma ne ne nimdeɛ te sɛ nkyene ne asaw.
Third Example Sentence: Yɛn adamfofa no yɛ nkyene ne asaw.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional clause; subject + negative verb + main clause; fixed proverb.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: If someone does not know, another will teach.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: People can learn from each other.
First Example Sentence: Ɔkyerɛkyerɛni no kae sɛ obi nnim a, obi kyere.
Second Example Sentence: Yɛyɛ adwuma no bom efisɛ obi nnim a, obi kyere.
Third Example Sentence: Sɛ wuntumi a, kae sɛ obi nnim a, obi kyere.
